{"command":"mkdir -p workspace && cat << 'EOF' > workspace/script.py
import urllib.request
import urllib.parse
import json
import re
import time
import sys

def get_bibtex_from_crossref(query):
    url = f"https://api.crossref.org/works?query.title={urllib.parse.quote(query)}&select=DOI,title&rows=3"
    try:
        req = urllib.request.Request(url, headers={'User-Agent': 'mailto:test@example.com'})
        response = urllib.request.urlopen(req)
        data = json.loads(response.read().decode('utf-8'))
        items = data.get('message', {}).get('items', [])
        
        for item in items:
            title = item.get('title', [''])[0]
            # Simple check if title roughly matches
            if query.lower().split()[:3] == title.lower().split()[:3] or query.lower()[:20] in title.lower():
                doi = item.get('DOI')
                if doi:
                    bib_url = f"https://api.crossref.org/works/{doi}/transform/application/x-bibtex"
                    req_bib = urllib.request.Request(bib_url, headers={'User-Agent': 'mailto:test@example.com'})
                    resp_bib = urllib.request.urlopen(req_bib)
                    return resp_bib.read().decode('utf-8')
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error querying CrossRef for '{query}': {e}", file=sys.stderr)
    return None

def get_bibtex_from_semanticscholar(query):
    url = f"https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/search?query={urllib.parse.quote(query)}&limit=3&fields=title,authors,venue,year,volume,issue,pages,externalIds"
    try:
        req = urllib.request.Request(url, headers={'User-Agent': 'Mozilla/5.0'})
        response = urllib.request.urlopen(req)
        data = json.loads(response.read().decode('utf-8'))
        items = data.get('data', [])
        
        for item in items:
            title = item.get('title', '')
            if query.lower()[:20] in title.lower() or query.lower().split()[:3] == title.lower().split()[:3]:
                # Attempt to get DOI and use crossref
                doi = item.get('externalIds', {}).get('DOI')
                if doi:
                    bib_url = f"https://api.crossref.org/works/{doi}/transform/application/x-bibtex"
                    try:
                        req_bib = urllib.request.Request(bib_url, headers={'User-Agent': 'mailto:test@example.com'})
                        resp_bib = urllib.request.urlopen(req_bib)
                        return resp_bib.read().decode('utf-8')
                    except:
                        pass
                
                # Manual construction if no DOI
                authors = " and ".join([a.get('name', '') for a in item.get('authors', [])])
                venue = item.get('venue', '')
                year = item.get('year', '')
                paper_id = item.get('paperId', 'id')
                
                bib = f"@article{{{paper_id},\n"
                bib += f"  title={{{title}}},\n"
                bib += f"  author={{{authors}}},\n"
                if venue: bib += f"  journal={{{venue}}},\n"
                if year: bib += f"  year={{{year}}},\n"
                if item.get('volume'): bib += f"  volume={{{item.get('volume')}}},\n"
                if item.get('issue'): bib += f"  number={{{item.get('issue')}}},\n"
                if item.get('pages'): bib += f"  pages={{{item.get('pages')}}},\n"
                bib += "}\n"
                return bib
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error querying Semantic Scholar for '{query}': {e}", file=sys.stderr)
    return None

def main():
    with open('context/paper_title.md', 'r') as f:
        lines = f.readlines()
        
    papers = []
    for line in lines:
        line = line.strip()
        if not line: continue
        # Remove [1] prefix
        match = re.match(r'\[\d+\]\s*(.*)', line)
        if match:
            papers.append(match.group(1))
        else:
            papers.append(line)
            
    # Task says "41 papers listed in paper_title.md" but the file has 65.
    # We will process up to 41.
    papers = papers[:41]
    
    with open('ref.bib', 'w') as out:
        for i, paper in enumerate(papers):
            print(f"[{i+1}/{len(papers)}] Fetching: {paper}")
            bib = get_bibtex_from_crossref(paper)
            if not bib:
                time.sleep(1)
                bib = get_bibtex_from_semanticscholar(paper)
            
            if bib:
                out.write(bib + "\n\n")
            else:
                print(f"FAILED to find bib for: {paper}")
                # Write a placeholder
                out.write(f"@misc{{missing_{i+1},\n  title={{{paper}}}\n}}\n\n")
            
            time.sleep(0.5)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
EOF
python3 workspace/script.py
"}

    <title>Sim2Real Diffusion: Leveraging Foundation Vision Language Models for Adaptive Automated Driving</title>

    <summary>Simulation-based design, optimization, and validation of autonomous vehicles have proven to be crucial for their improvement over the years. Nevertheless, the ultimate measure of effectiveness is their successful transition from simulation to reality (sim2real). However, existing sim2real transfer methods struggle to address the autonomy-oriented requirements of balancing: (i) conditioned domain adaptation, (ii) robust performance with limited examples, (iii) modularity in handling multiple domain representations, and (iv) real-time performance. To alleviate these pain points, we present a unified framework for learning cross-domain adaptive representations through conditional latent diffusion for sim2real transferable automated driving. Our framework offers options to leverage: (i) alternate foundation models, (ii) a few-shot fine-tuning pipeline, and (iii) textual as well as image prompts for mapping across given source and target domains. It is also capable of generating diverse high-quality samples when diffusing across parameter spaces such as times of day, weather conditions, seasons, and operational design domains. We systematically analyze the presented framework and report our findings in terms of performance benchmarks and ablation studies. Additionally, we demonstrate its serviceability for autonomous driving using behavioral cloning case studies. Our experiments indicate that the proposed framework is capable of bridging the perceptual sim2real gap by over 40%.</summary>

    <summary>Telepresence robots enable users to interact with remote environments, but efficient and intuitive navigation remains a challenge. In this work, we developed and evaluated a shared control method, in which the robot navigates autonomously while allowing users to affect the path generation to better suit their needs. We compared this with control switching, where users toggle between direct and automated control. We hypothesized that shared control would maintain efficiency comparable to control switching while potentially reducing user workload. The results of two consecutive user studies (each with final sample of n=20) showed that shared control does not degrade navigation efficiency, but did not show a significant reduction in task load compared to control switching. Further research is needed to explore the underlying factors that influence user preference and performance in these control systems.</summary>

    <summary>This manuscript presents a control barrier function based approach to shared control for preventing a vehicle from entering the part of the state space where it is unrecoverable. The maximal phase recoverable ellipse is presented as a safe set in the sideslip angle--yaw rate phase plane where the vehicle's state can be maintained. An exponential control barrier function is then defined on the maximal phase recoverable ellipse to promote safety.     <summary>Subsystems that are coupled due to dynamics and costs arise naturally in various communication applications. In many such applications the control actions are shared between different control stations giving rise to a \emph{control sharing} information structure. Previous studies of control-sharing have concentrated on the linear quadratic Gaussian setup and a solution approach tailored to continuous valued control actions. In this paper a three step solution approach for finite valued control actions is presented. In the first step, a person-by-person approach is used to identify redundant data or a sufficient statistic for local information at each control station. In the second step, the common-information based approach of Nayyar et al.\ (2011) is used to find a sufficient statistic for the common information shared between all control stations and to obtain a dynamic programming decomposition. In the third step, the specifics of the model are used to simplify the sufficient statistic and the dynamic program. As an example, an exact solution of a two-user multiple access broadcast system is presented.</summary>

import urllib.request
import urllib.parse
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET

def search_arxiv(query):
    url = f"https://export.arxiv.org/api/query?search_query=ti:%22{urllib.parse.quote(query)}%22&start=0&max_results=1"
    try:
        req = urllib.request.Request(url, headers={'User-Agent': 'Mozilla/5.0'})
        response = urllib.request.urlopen(req)
        root = ET.fromstring(response.read().decode('utf-8'))
        namespace = {'atom': 'http://www.w3.org/2005/Atom'}
        for entry in root.findall('atom:entry', namespace):
            title = entry.find('atom:title', namespace).text.replace('\n', ' ')
            authors = [a.find('atom:name', namespace).text for a in entry.findall('atom:author', namespace)]
            author_str = " and ".join(authors)
            published = entry.find('atom:published', namespace).text
            year = published[:4]
            arxiv_id = entry.find('atom:id', namespace).text.split('/abs/')[-1]
            
            bib = f"@article{{arxiv_{arxiv_id.replace('.', '_')},\n"
            bib += f"  title={{{title}}},\n"
            bib += f"  author={{{author_str}}},\n"
            bib += f"  journal={{arXiv preprint arXiv:{arxiv_id}}},\n"
            bib += f"  year={{{year}}},\n"
            bib += "}\n"
            return bib
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error: {e}")
    return None

for line in text.split('\n'):
    line = line.strip()
    if not in_bib and line.startswith('@'):
        in_bib = True
        current.append(line)
        brace_count += line.count('{') - line.count('}')
    elif in_bib:
        current.append(line)
        brace_count += line.count('{') - line.count('}')
        if brace_count == 0:
            blocks.append("\n".join(current))
            current = []
            in_bib = False

print(f"Total proper BibTeX entries found: {len(blocks)}")
for i, b in enumerate(blocks):
    match = re.search(r'title\s*=\s*\{([^\}]+)\}', b, re.IGNORECASE)
    title = match.group(1) if match else "UNKNOWN"
    print(f"[{i+1}] {title[:80]}")

import urllib.request
import urllib.parse
import json
import re
import time
import sys

def get_bibtex_from_crossref(query):
    url = f"https://api.crossref.org/works?query.title={urllib.parse.quote(query)}&select=DOI,title&rows=3"
    try:
        req = urllib.request.Request(url, headers={'User-Agent': 'mailto:test@example.com'})
        response = urllib.request.urlopen(req)
        data = json.loads(response.read().decode('utf-8'))
        items = data.get('message', {}).get('items', [])
        
        for item in items:
            title = item.get('title', [''])[0]
            # Simple check if title roughly matches
            if query.lower().split()[:3] == title.lower().split()[:3] or query.lower()[:20] in title.lower():
                doi = item.get('DOI')
                if doi:
                    bib_url = f"https://api.crossref.org/works/{doi}/transform/application/x-bibtex"
                    req_bib = urllib.request.Request(bib_url, headers={'User-Agent': 'mailto:test@example.com'})
                    resp_bib = urllib.request.urlopen(req_bib)
                    return resp_bib.read().decode('utf-8')
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error querying CrossRef for '{query}': {e}", file=sys.stderr)
    return None

def get_bibtex_from_semanticscholar(query):
    url = f"https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/search?query={urllib.parse.quote(query)}&limit=3&fields=title,authors,venue,year,volume,issue,pages,externalIds"
    try:
        req = urllib.request.Request(url, headers={'User-Agent': 'Mozilla/5.0'})
        response = urllib.request.urlopen(req)
        data = json.loads(response.read().decode('utf-8'))
        items = data.get('data', [])
        
        for item in items:
            title = item.get('title', '')
            if query.lower()[:20] in title.lower() or query.lower().split()[:3] == title.lower().split()[:3]:
                # Attempt to get DOI and use crossref
                doi = item.get('externalIds', {}).get('DOI')
                if doi:
                    bib_url = f"https://api.crossref.org/works/{doi}/transform/application/x-bibtex"
                    try:
                        req_bib = urllib.request.Request(bib_url, headers={'User-Agent': 'mailto:test@example.com'})
                        resp_bib = urllib.request.urlopen(req_bib)
                        return resp_bib.read().decode('utf-8')
                    except:
                        pass
                
                # Manual construction if no DOI
                authors = " and ".join([a.get('name', '') for a in item.get('authors', [])])
                venue = item.get('venue', '')
                year = item.get('year', '')
                paper_id = item.get('paperId', 'id')
                
                bib = f"@article{{{paper_id},\n"
                bib += f"  title={{{title}}},\n"
                bib += f"  author={{{authors}}},\n"
                if venue: bib += f"  journal={{{venue}}},\n"
                if year: bib += f"  year={{{year}}},\n"
                if item.get('volume'): bib += f"  volume={{{item.get('volume')}}},\n"
                if item.get('issue'): bib += f"  number={{{item.get('issue')}}},\n"
                if item.get('pages'): bib += f"  pages={{{item.get('pages')}}},\n"
                bib += "}\n"
                return bib
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error querying Semantic Scholar for '{query}': {e}", file=sys.stderr)
    return None

def main():
    with open('context/paper_title.md', 'r') as f:
        lines = f.readlines()
        
    papers = []
    for line in lines:
        line = line.strip()
        if not line: continue
        # Remove [1] prefix
        match = re.match(r'\[\d+\]\s*(.*)', line)
        if match:
            papers.append(match.group(1))
        else:
            papers.append(line)
            
    # Task says "41 papers listed in paper_title.md" but the file has 65.
    # We will process up to 41.
    papers = papers[:41]
    
    with open('ref.bib', 'w') as out:
        for i, paper in enumerate(papers):
            print(f"[{i+1}/{len(papers)}] Fetching: {paper}")
            bib = get_bibtex_from_crossref(paper)
            if not bib:
                time.sleep(1)
                bib = get_bibtex_from_semanticscholar(paper)
            
            if bib:
                out.write(bib + "\n\n")
            else:
                print(f"FAILED to find bib for: {paper}")
                # Write a placeholder
                out.write(f"@misc{{missing_{i+1},\n  title={{{paper}}}\n}}\n\n")
            
            time.sleep(0.5)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

    <title>Sim2Real Diffusion: Leveraging Foundation Vision Language Models for Adaptive Automated Driving</title>
    <updated>2025-10-31T03:13:58Z</updated>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.00236v3" rel="alternate" type="text/html"/>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.00236v3" rel="related" type="application/pdf" title="pdf"/>
    <summary>Simulation-based design, optimization, and validation of autonomous vehicles have proven to be crucial for their improvement over the years. Nevertheless, the ultimate measure of effectiveness is their successful transition from simulation to reality (sim2real). However, existing sim2real transfer methods struggle to address the autonomy-oriented requirements of balancing: (i) conditioned domain adaptation, (ii) robust performance with limited examples, (iii) modularity in handling multiple domain representations, and (iv) real-time performance. To alleviate these pain points, we present a unified framework for learning cross-domain adaptive representations through conditional latent diffusion for sim2real transferable automated driving. Our framework offers options to leverage: (i) alternate foundation models, (ii) a few-shot fine-tuning pipeline, and (iii) textual as well as image prompts for mapping across given source and target domains. It is also capable of generating diverse high-quality samples when diffusing across parameter spaces such as times of day, weather conditions, seasons, and operational design domains. We systematically analyze the presented framework and report our findings in terms of performance benchmarks and ablation studies. Additionally, we demonstrate its serviceability for autonomous driving using behavioral cloning case studies. Our experiments indicate that the proposed framework is capable of bridging the perceptual sim2real gap by over 40%.</summary>

    <summary>Telepresence robots enable users to interact with remote environments, but efficient and intuitive navigation remains a challenge. In this work, we developed and evaluated a shared control method, in which the robot navigates autonomously while allowing users to affect the path generation to better suit their needs. We compared this with control switching, where users toggle between direct and automated control. We hypothesized that shared control would maintain efficiency comparable to control switching while potentially reducing user workload. The results of two consecutive user studies (each with final sample of n=20) showed that shared control does not degrade navigation efficiency, but did not show a significant reduction in task load compared to control switching. Further research is needed to explore the underlying factors that influence user preference and performance in these control systems.</summary>

    <summary>This manuscript presents a control barrier function based approach to shared control for preventing a vehicle from entering the part of the state space where it is unrecoverable. The maximal phase recoverable ellipse is presented as a safe set in the sideslip angle--yaw rate phase plane where the vehicle's state can be maintained. An exponential control barrier function is then defined on the maximal phase recoverable ellipse to promote safety. Simulations demonstrate that this approach enables safe drifting, that is, driving at the handling limit without spinning out. Results are then validated for shared control drifting with an experimental vehicle in a closed course. The results show the ability of this shared control formulation to maintain the vehicle's state within a safe domain in a computationally efficient manner, even in extreme drifting maneuvers.</summary>

    <title>Optimal decentralized control of coupled subsystems with control sharing</title>
    <updated>2012-08-07T21:52:03Z</updated>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/abs/1112.6220v2" rel="alternate" type="text/html"/>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/1112.6220v2" rel="related" type="application/pdf" title="pdf"/>
    <summary>Subsystems that are coupled due to dynamics and costs arise naturally in various communication applications. In many such applications the control actions are shared between different control stations giving rise to a \emph{control sharing} information structure. Previous studies of control-sharing have concentrated on the linear quadratic Gaussian setup and a solution approach tailored to continuous valued control actions. In this paper a three step solution approach for finite valued control actions is presented. In the first step, a person-by-person approach is used to identify redundant data or a sufficient statistic for local information at each control station. In the second step, the common-information based approach of Nayyar et al.\ (2011) is used to find a sufficient statistic for the common information shared between all control stations and to obtain a dynamic programming decomposition. In the third step, the specifics of the model are used to simplify the sufficient statistic and the dynamic program. As an example, an exact solution of a two-user multiple access broadcast system is presented.</summary>

{"command":"cat << 'EOF' > workspace/count_at.py
import re

with open('ref.bib', 'r') as f:
    text = f.read()

# We only care about root-level @...{...} patterns 
blocks = []
current = []
brace_count = 0
in_bib = False

for line in text.split('\n'):
    line = line.strip()
    if not in_bib and line.startswith('@'):
        in_bib = True
        current.append(line)
        brace_count += line.count('{') - line.count('}')
    elif in_bib:
        current.append(line)
        brace_count += line.count('{') - line.count('}')
        if brace_count == 0:
            blocks.append("\n".join(current))
            current = []
            in_bib = False

print(f"Total proper BibTeX entries found: {len(blocks)}")
for i, b in enumerate(blocks):
    match = re.search(r'title\s*=\s*\{([^\}]+)\}', b, re.IGNORECASE)
    title = match.group(1) if match else "UNKNOWN"
    print(f"[{i+1}] {title[:80]}")

EOF
python3 workspace/count_at.py"}
